description: The most widespread oak in southern California, forming dense woodlands along the coast and interior valleys. Iconic evergreen tree with distinctive holly-like leaves.
range: Sea level to 1,500m; coastal valleys and foothills from Mendocino County to Baja California
habitat: Coastal oak woodland, chaparral fringe, valley bottoms
leafDescription: Evergreen, convex (holly-like), 2–5cm, spine-toothed margins, dark glossy green above, paler below
acornDescription: Slender, cylindrical, 2–3cm, cap covers 1/3 of nut, matures in first year
barkDescription: Smooth gray when young, becoming deeply furrowed and dark brown with age
fireResponse: Highly fire-tolerant; resprouts vigorously from crown and root crown after fire

description: A rare and beautiful semi-deciduous oak restricted to the Peninsular Ranges and Santa Rosa Plateau. One of California's rarest oaks with a limited range in San Diego and Riverside counties.
range: Restricted to Peninsular Ranges; San Diego County north to Riverside County; also Baja California
habitat: Foothill woodland, grassland-oak savanna on gentle slopes
leafDescription: Semi-deciduous, oblong to elliptic, 3–6cm, margins entire or wavy, blue-green above
acornDescription: Oblong, 1.5–2.5cm, cap covers 1/2 of nut, matures in first year
fireResponse: Moderate tolerance; resprouts from crown after low-intensity fire

description: California's largest native oak and one of the most majestic trees in North America. Deciduous with deeply lobed leaves and massive spreading canopy.
range: Interior valleys and foothills throughout California; reaches southern limit in Los Angeles and San Diego counties
habitat: Valley floors, gentle slopes, deep alluvial soils near streams
leafDescription: Deciduous, deeply 7–11 lobed, 5–10cm, bright green above, paler below
acornDescription: Long, cylindrical, 3–5cm, tapered tip, cap warty and covers 1/4 of nut, matures in first year
barkDescription: Pale gray, deeply checkered and ridged
fireResponse: Moderate tolerance; thick bark protects mature trees; resprouts after fire

description: The most widespread oak in California by elevation. Highly adaptable evergreen oak found in rocky canyons and mountain slopes throughout SoCal ranges.
range: Mountains and canyons throughout California; most elevation-tolerant SoCal oak
habitat: Rocky canyon slopes, chaparral-forest transition, riparian canyons
leafDescription: Evergreen, variable — flat to convex, 2–8cm, margins entire or spine-toothed, golden-hairy below when young
acornDescription: Broadly ovoid, 1.5–3cm, distinctive golden-yellow cup with thick scales, matures in second year
barkDescription: Light gray, scaly to somewhat furrowed
fireResponse: Very fire-tolerant; thick bark and prolific crown sprouting; persists after severe fire

description: A stiff-leaved evergreen oak of drier interior foothills and mixed woodland zones. Intermediate between coast live and scrub oak in form.
range: Interior foothills and lower mountain slopes; widespread in California interior
habitat: Mixed woodland, chaparral borders, drier north-facing slopes
leafDescription: Evergreen, flat to slightly cupped, 2–6cm, margins entire to spine-toothed, glossy dark green above
acornDescription: Slender cylindrical, 2–4cm, matures in second year
fireResponse: Moderate tolerance; resprouts from root crown after fire

description: A dense, spiny evergreen shrub-oak dominating chaparral throughout SoCal. Often forms impenetrable thickets; key fire-adapted chaparral shrub.
range: Widespread chaparral throughout California; abundant in SoCal mountains and foothills
habitat: Chaparral, coastal sage scrub margins, rocky slopes
leafDescription: Evergreen, small 1–3cm, stiff, holly-like with spine-tipped lobes, dull green above
acornDescription: Small, ovoid, 1–2cm, cup covers 1/3 of nut, matures in first year
barkDescription: Grayish, rough and furrowed even on young stems
fireResponse: Fire-obligate chaparral shrub; resprouts vigorously from root crown (obligate resprouter)

description: An endemic island oak found only on the California Channel Islands. Evergreen with distinctive woolly leaf undersides. A relict species of great conservation interest.
range: Channel Islands only (Santa Cruz, Santa Rosa, Santa Catalina, San Clemente, Guadalupe Island)
habitat: Island woodlands, north-facing canyon slopes, mixed island chaparral
leafDescription: Evergreen, oblong to ovate, 4–10cm, margins entire to wavy, distinctively woolly/tomentose beneath
acornDescription: Ovoid, 1.5–3cm, tomentose cup covers 1/3–1/2 of nut, matures in second year
fireResponse: Limited data; island fire regimes differ from mainland

description: Named for its distinctive blue-gray foliage. Deciduous foothill oak most abundant in Central Valley foothills; reaches its southern range limit in Los Angeles and San Bernardino counties.
range: Primarily Central Valley foothills; southern limit in Los Angeles and San Bernardino counties
habitat: Dry foothill woodland, open savannas, rocky slopes
leafDescription: Deciduous, oblong, 3–7cm, shallowly lobed or toothed, distinctive blue-gray color
acornDescription: Ovoid, 1.5–3cm, shallow cup covers 1/4 of nut, matures in first year
fireResponse: Moderate to high tolerance; thick bark protects mature trees on dry sites
